title: Wave
intro: Product documentation for setup, operation, integration, development, troubleshooting, and support.

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave is a high-performance dexterous hand designed for research and advanced robotic manipulation. It enables precise interaction with tools and objects, supporting a wide range of complex tasks and experimental applications.

items.text: 22 active degrees of freedom for highly dexterous manipulation
Human-like size and kinematics for intuitive interaction and control
High-resolution tactile sensing at each fingertip
Compliant joints for safe and adaptive contact
High reliability for continuous operation

blocks.text: Joint names follow standard anatomical conventions:

items.text: Thumb: CMC, MCP, IP
Fingers (index, middle, ring): MCP, PIP, DIP
Pinky: CMC, MCP, PIP, DIP

blocks.text: The following abbreviations are used consistently in this manual and in SDK parameter names:

items.text: FE: Flexion–Extension
AA: Abduction–Adduction
Read this manual before operating the product
Use the device only as intended
Do not disassemble the product unless explicitly permitted by Sharpa
Always follow proper installation and operation procedures

rows.text:
The product enclosure may become hot during or after operation.
Avoid direct skin contact
Allow at least 30 minutes for cooling after power-off
Keep flammable materials away

Moving joints may create pinch hazards.
Keep hands and fingers away from joint gaps during operation
Do not place body parts near moving components

items.text: Ensure all connectors are clean and dry before powering on
Do not use damaged cables or connectors
Use the provided power adapter or ensure compliance with specifications
Avoid contact with sharp objects
Maximum limits:
Pressure: < 2 MPa
Normal force: < 50 N
Sliding force: < 30 N
The product should be operated by personnel with an engineering background
Follow all instructions during operation
Contact Sharpa for technical support if needed

blocks.text: This product may emit electromagnetic fields.

items.text: If you use medical devices (e.g., pacemakers), consult a physician before use
Stop using the product if interference is suspected

blocks.text: Do not use the product in:

items.text: Explosive or flammable environments
Corrosive chemical environments
High humidity or liquid exposure conditions (IP20 rated)
Temperature: 0–50°C
Humidity: < 60% RH
Do not drop, crush, or burn the product
If the enclosure is damaged, stop using immediately
Use proper packaging during transportation

blocks.text: Stop using the product immediately if:

items.text: Abnormal noise or vibration is detected
The product appears damaged
Nearby equipment malfunctions
Any person experiences discomfort

blocks.text: Contact Sharpa Technical Support for assistance at support@sharpa.com.
① Peak power: 180 W (10 ms transient); average power: 100 W.

items.text: Direction Definition
FE (Flexion–Extension)
Positive: toward the palm (flexion)
Negative: away from the palm (extension)
AA (Abduction–Adduction)
Positive: toward the pinky side
Negative: toward the thumb side
Zero position definition
Defined as the neutral pose after calibration, as shown below:
Joint Limits
MCP FE below -10° is reserved for collision avoidance

blocks.text: ① Total error, specified at 25 °C after zero calibration.

rows.text:
6-DOF force
Deformation map
Contact point
Raw image

Standard Mode: 30 Hz (raw image stream & processed outputs)
High-Performance Mode: up to 180 Hz (raw image stream)

blocks.text: ① Maximum load: Maximum allowable force without causing sensor damage

items.text: Ensure the product is properly installed and placed on a stable surface
Read the Safety Instructions carefully
Prepare a host PC for running Sharpa Pilot app
Attach the flange adapter to the base using M6 screws (diagonal tightening recommended)
Place the hand onto the adapter
Secure the hand using M4 screws
Put the assembled unit on a flat, stable workspace
Ensure power is OFF
Connect the wrist cable
Connect one end to the hand and the other end to the connection box
Align the connector key with the socket
Insert along the axis
Tighten the coupling nut until fully locked

blocks.text:
Ensure proper alignment before inserting the wrist connector
Do not force insertion
Avoid pulling or bending the cable excessively

items.text: Connect the Ethernet cable
Connect one end to the connection box and the other end to the computer
Connect the power adapter
Connect the power adapter to the connection box, then plug it into a power outlet
The rear hand indicator turns cyan (breathing) → device initialized
The device becomes discoverable by the host PC

blocks.text: During startup, keep fingertips free from contact

items.text: OS: Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04

blocks.text: Download Sharpa Pilot from https://www.sharpa.com/pages/download and install:
This section summarizes the factory default network settings of Sharpa Wave and explains how to connect the device to the host PC.

Use these values for first-time setup, device discovery, troubleshooting, and recovery after a reset. If the network settings have been intentionally changed, use the current configured values instead.
Configure the host PC according to the values in Section 5.1, Default Network Settings.

items.text: Ensure that no other device on the network is using the same IP address as the host PC or Sharpa Wave.
For instructions on changing the device IP address or tactile destination IP, see Sharpa Pilot.

blocks.text: Joint calibration establishes the zero reference used by the control system. It is recommended each time the device is powered on to ensure accurate joint control.

items.text: the hand is securely mounted;
the workspace is free of obstacles;
no person is in contact with the hand.
Launch Sharpa Pilot.
Wait until the device card appears.
Click the device card.
Open the Calib panel and click Start Calibration.
Wait for the automatic calibration sequence to complete. The hand will return to the zero position.
Confirm that the calibration completion message appears (~95 seconds).
After calibration, the system reports joint backlash variation.
Joints with a deviation greater than 1.5° are highlighted.
Check these joints before operation to ensure that they do not affect performance.
Open Sliders panel in Sharpa Pilot
Drag sliders to move joints
Go to Replay panel
Select a preset motion
Click Play

rows.text: Used to mount the hand onto a base or robot interface
Provides stable support for bench-top operation
Used for installation and fastening screws
Provides power input and Ethernet communication interface

Used for network connection between device and PC
Connects the hand to the connection box (length: 2 m)

blocks.text: This section describes how the hand is controlled, what information it provides, and how to operate it effectively. It covers control logic, state feedback, command behavior, joint definitions, and a basic operation workflow.
This section defines how control is managed in the system, including control sources and the rules that govern command execution.
The system supports the following control sources. Each source represents a different way to control the hand and provides different capabilities.

rows.text:
GUI-based interface for control, monitoring, and configuration
Manual control via sliders and motion replay
Real-time visualization of joint states and tactile data
Configuration of control source, control mode, and parameters
Calibration tools for joints and tactile sensors
Fault detection and system status monitoring

Programmatic interface providing full access to all control and monitoring capabilities
Send joint commands and execute motion control
Read joint states and tactile data
Configure control source, control mode, and system parameters
Perform calibration procedures
Monitor device status and fault information
Integrate with external systems and control algorithms

items.text: Only one control source can send motion commands at a time
Motion commands from other sources are ignored
Configuration commands remain valid across sources
When set to IDLE:
All motion commands are ignored
The hand remains non-actuated

blocks.text:
Ensure only one active controller is sending motion commands.
Conflicts may result in unexpected motion.
The hand provides real-time feedback on joint states via Sharpa Pilot or the SDK.

rows.text:
① Torque Source
By default, joint torque feedback is estimated from motor current.
For the MCP joints of the four fingers (FE and AA) and the CMC joints of the thumb (FE and AA), torque feedback can alternatively be obtained from integrated force sensors.
The torque source can be switched in Sharpa Pilot (Hardware Settings → Admittance Parameters → Force Feedback Source).
When switching to sensor-based torque feedback, force sensor calibration must be performed beforehand. Calibration can be completed in Sharpa Pilot (Hardware Settings → Force Sensor Calibration).

blocks.text: The hand supports multiple control modes, each designed for different types of tasks and interaction patterns.
Selecting the appropriate mode is essential for achieving stable and effective behavior.

rows.text: Tracks target joint angles with high accuracy; resists disturbances
Motion adapts to external force; compliant interaction
Controls torque via stiffness and damping; stable contact interaction
Backdrivable; can be moved freely by external forces

items.text: Control modes can be switched at runtime via Sharpa Pilot or Sharpa Wave SDK
Always ensure the correct control source is active before sending commands
Calibration is recommended before executing precise motion
For smooth motion, avoid large discontinuities in target positions

blocks.text: Joint commands and state arrays follow a fixed index order:
The hand can be controlled programmatically via the SDK, which provides full access to motion control, state feedback, and system configuration.
The SDK is available in C++ and Python. For detailed API definitions and examples, refer to the Sharpa Wave SDK.

items.text: Ensure the hand is properly connected and powered on
Ensure the control source is set to SDK (via Sharpa Pilot or API)
Download Sharpa Wave SDK from https://www.sharpa.com/pages/download
Follow the instructions in README.md
Run the provided examples:
Python: SharpaWaveSDK_x.x.x/sample/python/sharpa_wave_example.py
C++: SharpaWaveSDK_x.x.x/sample/c++/sharpa_wave_example.cc

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave is equipped with vision-based tactile sensors at each fingertip. The sensors measure contact by capturing deformation of a compliant surface using an internal camera and processing it with onboard algorithms.
The tactile system provides real-time information about:

items.text: contact occurrence
contact force
contact location and distribution on the fingertip surface

blocks.text: Tactile data is streamed to the host system, where it may be processed either onboard or on the host depending on the selected mode.

Each fingertip provides multiple types of tactile data. These outputs describe the same contact event from different perspectives and can be used independently or jointly, depending on the application.

rows.text: Resultant contact force on the fingertip. Available in Sharpa Pilot and SDK
Detect contact, measure force magnitude or direction
Contact detection, force control, grasp monitoring
2D distribution of surface deformation. Available in Sharpa Pilot and SDK
Understand where and how contact occurs
Contact localization, contact area estimation, shape analysis
Estimated contact location on the fingertip surface, derived from deformation data. Available in Sharpa Pilot and SDK
Contact tracking, object pose estimation, control reference point
Original camera image inside the sensor. Available via SDK
Debugging, custom tactile algorithms, data-driven modeling

items.text: Use force as the primary signal for most applications.
Use deformation map when spatial contact distribution is required.
Use contact point when a compact representation of contact location is sufficient.
Use all outputs for data-driven or learning-based applications, and perform feature selection during model development.

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave supports two tactile processing modes:

items.text: Standard (Onboard) Mode: Tactile inference is performed on the device.
High-Performance (Host) Mode: Raw tactile data is streamed to the host, where inference is performed using GPU acceleration.

rows.text:
NVIDIA GPU required (RTX 3060+ recommended)
Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04
NVIDIA driver
NVIDIA Container Toolkit
Docker Engine

items.text: a simple and stable setup is preferred
no GPU is available
standard tactile feedback is sufficient
low-frequency data is sufficient (e.g., monitoring, logging, basic control)
higher temporal resolution is required
real-time or high-frequency processing is needed
advanced applications (e.g., learning or custom algorithms) require high-frequency data

blocks.text: The SDK supports two tactile data pipelines:

items.text: Standard mode: Standard SDK, no Docker required
High-Performance mode: Docker-based SDK with GPU acceleration

blocks.text: These modes use different SDK packages and deployment workflows.

Tactile data access via the SDK cannot be used simultaneously with Sharpa Pilot. This applies to both the Standard SDK and the Docker-based SDK. Both SDK pipelines and Sharpa Pilot use the same communication ports for tactile data streaming, which will result in conflicts. Close Sharpa Pilot before running any SDK-based tactile application.
In this mode, tactile data is processed inside the device and streamed to the host.
Use the standard Sharpa Wave SDK and run the provided examples:

items.text: Python
SharpaWaveSDK_x.x.x/sample/python/sharpa_tactile_fetch.py
SharpaWaveSDK_x.x.x/sample/python/sharpa_tactile_callback.py
SharpaWaveSDK_x.x.x/sample/c++/sharpa_tactile_example.cc

blocks.text: In this mode, the device streams raw tactile images to the host, and tactile inference is performed on the host using GPU acceleration via the Docker-based SDK.
This section describes how to deploy and run the high-performance tactile pipeline.

items.text: Install Docker and GPU Runtime

blocks.code:
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker

items.text: Prepare Docker Configuration
Create a docker-compose.yml file:

blocks.code:
services:
  tactile_dev:
    image: sharpadev/sharpawavesdk:<image tag>
    working_dir: /root
    container_name: sharpa_dev
    runtime: nvidia
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [ gpu ]
    network_mode: host
    privileged: true
    volumes:
      - /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix
    environment:
      - DISPLAY=${DISPLAY}
      - QT_X11_NO_MITSHM=1
    cap_add:
      - SYS_ADMIN
    devices:
      - /dev/fuse:/dev/fuse
    security_opt:
      - apparmor:unconfined
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    restart: always

items.text: Select and Pull Docker Image
here we take the docker image sharpadev/sharpawavesdk:5.0.0.8b7e for example, you can get the latest Docker image from https://hub.docker.com/r/sharpadev/sharpawavesdk
RTX 40 series + driver ~570:
RTX 50 series + driver ~580:
Start Docker Container

blocks.code:
docker stop sharpa_dev && docker rm sharpa_dev
xhost +local:root
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml up -d

items.text: Enter Docker Container
Configure Tactile Data Rate

blocks.text: The tactile data rate is configured by editing the following file inside the Docker container:

rows.text:
Tactile data rate
Type: int (must be integer)

blocks.text: Ensure the following parameters are set:

blocks.code:
{
  ... ...
  ... ...
  ... ...
  "left": {
    ... ...
    ... ...
    "fps": 180,
    "infer_from_device": false,
    "require_jpeg": true,
    "decode_jpeg": true
    ... ...
    ... ...
  },
  "right": {
    ... ...
    ... ...
    "fps": 180,
    "infer_from_device": false,
    "require_jpeg": true,
    "decode_jpeg": true
    ... ...
    ... ...
  }
}

blocks.text: In older versions of tactile.json, the configuration is split into two sections:

items.text: "none": used by the Standard SDK
"cuda": used by the Docker-based SDK

blocks.text: For High-Performamce mode, modify only the parameters under "cuda".

items.text: Run High-Performance Tactile Pipeline

blocks.text: A window will appear. When you press the elastomer on a fingertip (e.g., the ring finger), the deformation map in the window will update accordingly.

In the figure, channels 0–4 correspond to the right hand tactile sensors. In this example, only the right hand is connected, so the left-hand channels appear black because no data is received. For channel indexing and definitions, see the Tactile Channel Mapping section.
Sharpa Pilot provides real-time visualization of tactile data for both Standard and High-Performance modes.

items.text: force vector
contact distribution overlaid on the hand model
contact point information
real-time force components (Fx, Fy, Fz) and resultant magnitude

blocks.text: By default, Sharpa Pilot uses the standard onboard tactile pipeline.

items.text: Open Sharpa Pilot
Enable “Texture” and “Force” in the top toolbar
Enable the real-time force components (Fx, Fy, Fz) and resultant magnitude plot
Apply physical contact to a fingertip
Observe changes in tactile display and force plot

blocks.text: Sharpa Pilot cannot directly read data from the Docker pipeline, to visualize High-Performance host-side inference, a ZMQ publisher is used to forward tactile data to Sharpa Pilot.

items.text: Disable Onboard Tactile Driver
Disable tactile driver
Restart Pilot if needed

blocks.text: This ensures Pilot does not use the 30 Hz onboard pipeline.

items.text: Start ZMQ Publisher

blocks.text: This script publishes tactile data via ZMQ to localhost.

items.text: Verify Visualization
Tactile visualization updates in real time (~180 Hz)
Force and texture views respond faster than 30 Hz mode
If No Data Appears
ZMQ script is running
No firewall blocks localhost ports
Restart Sharpa Pilot
Restart Docker container
Do not run tactile_180fps.py and Sharpa Pilot simultaneously, as they may use conflicting tactile pipelines.
Ensure that only one tactile pipeline is active at a time.

blocks.text: The sensing area is the region of the fingertip where tactile data is physically valid.

items.text: It corresponds to the textured surface (ridges and grooves) of each fingertip
All tactile outputs (force, deformation, raw image) are derived from this region

blocks.text: Only data within this area should be considered when interpreting contact (marked in dark color in the figure below).
The raw image is the original camera capture inside the tactile sensor.

items.text: Contains the most complete information about surface deformation
Serves as the underlying signal from which higher-level representations (e.g., deformation map and force) are derived
Includes visual patterns that may not be preserved in processed outputs

blocks.text: This representation is primarily useful when low-level sensor behavior or custom processing is required.
Raw image from the fingertip tactile sensor
Deformation depth is defined as the displacement of each surface point along its normal direction relative to the no-contact state.

items.text: The deformation map is a 2D grayscale image (240 × 240)
Each pixel corresponds to a 3D point on the fingertip surface
Pixel intensity represents deformation depth: brighter regions indicate larger deformation

blocks.text: The 2D representation is obtained by flattening the curved fingertip surface using an angle-preserving parameterization method (e.g., Angle-Based Flattening, ABF). This approach preserves local geometric structure while minimizing distortion introduced during the 3D-to-2D mapping.

items.text: Captures the spatial distribution of contact
Reflects:
contact area
pressure distribution
local shape interaction

blocks.text: Mapping Between Grayscale Value and Deformation Depth
To balance high sensitivity and a wide measurement range, the deformation depth map is encoded as a grayscale image.
Each pixel value corresponds to the deformation depth of the associated surface point.

items.text: Lower grayscale values represent small or no deformation
Higher grayscale values represent larger deformation

blocks.text: The mapping between grayscale value and deformation depth is nonlinear, as illustrated in the figure below.

items.text: high sensitivity in small deformation ranges
extended dynamic range for larger deformation

blocks.text: For precise numerical conversion, refer to the Sharpa Wave SDK.
The contact point represents a single representative location of contact between the fingertip sensor and the object.
It is typically computed from the deformation map as the weighted centroid of the contact region.

items.text: The contact point is represented as the 2D coordinates [column, row] in the deformation map
Multiple contact points can be detected when there are multiple separated contact regions
The contact point provides a compact representation of the contact location

blocks.text: It is typically located near the center of the deformation region, making it useful for tracking and control applications.
representing the resultant external force and torque acting on the entire sensing surface.

items.text: Force unit: Newton (N)
Torque unit: Newton-meter (Nm)

blocks.text: The force is defined in a left-handed coordinate system.

items.text: center of the IP joint's rotation axis (thumb)
center of the DIP joint's rotation axis (index, middle, ring, and pinky fingers)

blocks.text: This definition is consistent with the SDK outputs and the URDF model.

items.text: The output represents the global effect of contact, not local distribution
Multiple contact points are aggregated into a single resultant wrench
Torque components reflect the moment induced by off-center contact

blocks.text: This representation is suitable for understanding overall interaction but does not capture spatial details of contact.
Each pixel in the deformation map corresponds to a 3D point on the fingertip surface.

items.text: 2D image space (pixel coordinates)
3D physical space (surface geometry)
Enables reconstruction of contact in 3D space
Allows deformation data to be interpreted in physically meaningful coordinates
Supports integration with geometric models and simulation environments

blocks.text: Most applications do not require explicit use of this mapping.
It becomes relevant when precise geometric reasoning or reconstruction is needed.
The 2D–3D mapping can be obtained in two ways:

items.text: Precomputed sensor model assets: Check the Assets/tactile_sensor_digital_model folder in the downloading resource.
NPY files:

blocks.text: Since the 2D image contains  pixels, each of the above files contains a matrix of size.

items.text: OBJ files (used for visualizing the sensor surface shape):

blocks.text:
Line starting with v: 3D point coordinates (x, y, z)
Line starting with vt: Normalized 2D point coordinates (row, column)
Line starting with vn: Normal vector (nx, ny, nz) of a 3D point

items.text: SDK interfaces: Use the SDK to obtain the 3D point's normal vector (nx, ny, nz), which corresponds to the 2D point coordinates (row, column).
See the "Tactile Data Acquisition" section in Sharpa Wave SDK.

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave is designed for flexible integration across a wide range of robotic platforms, from rapid prototyping setups to fully integrated systems.
It supports multiple mechanical and electrical interface combinations, enabling:

items.text: Fast integration using standardized interfaces and external cabling
Compact integration with internal routing and minimal wrist length
Flange: Adapts to standard robot interfaces via a flange adapter
Wrist: Connects directly to the robot wrist using screws; requires a custom adapter
Internal: Integrates via an internal pogo pin interface, replacing the wrist module

blocks.text: The following diagram illustrates the relative positions of the three interface options:

items.text: Wrist connector: External aviation connector for power and communication
Internal pin pad: Pogo pin interface for internal power and communication
Requires a custom 3 × 6 pogo pin array for mating (not a standard connector)

rows.text:
Standard robot flange interfaces (e.g., UR, xArm, Franka, Flexiv)
fast and reliable integration

Standardized compatibility
simple and robust mounting

Custom robot interfaces
rapid prototyping with a custom adapter

Flexible for custom designs
easier prototyping

Additional mechanical design effort
external cabling
longer wrist length

Compact integrated designs
minimal wrist length; internal cable routing

Shortest wrist length
clean integrated routing

High alignment precision required
Requires custom pogo pin array for electrical connection
More complex mechanical and electrical integration

items.text: Flange
Wrist
Internal
Hand weight: 1.3 kg
Recommended robot payload ≥ 5 kg
Voltage: 18–28 V
Maximum power consumption:
Single hand: 100 W average, 180 W peak
Dual hand: 200 W average, 360 W peak
Ethernet (1000BASE-T) is required for 180 Hz tactile data streaming.
Ethernet (100BASE-TX) is sufficient for hand control and 30 Hz tactile data.
Typical bandwidth usage (per hand):
30 Hz (processed outputs): ~10 Mbps
30 Hz (processed + raw image): ~40 Mbps
180 Hz (raw image): ~125 Mbps

blocks.text: Two network setups are supported for dual-hand configuration: using a network switch (recommended) or using multiple Ethernet interfaces.

items.text: Using a network switch (recommended):
Connect the host and both devices via a switch.
Use a single host IP (e.g., 192.168.10.240) as the tactile destination IP.
Ensure all devices are on the same network segment.
Using multiple Ethernet interfaces:
Each host interface must have a unique IP address.
Each hand and the corresponding host interface must be on the same subnet.
Each hand must be configured with a tactile destination IP matching the corresponding host interface.

blocks.text: The connector is located at the wrist on the back of the hand. Pin arrangement is shown below:

rows.text:
Pin No.	Signal name	Description
1, 2	PWR	Power input (positive)
8, 9	GND	Power input (negative)
12	MDI0_N	1000BASE-T Ethernet
10	MDI0_P	
6	MDI1_N	
7	MDI1_P	
4	MDI2_N	
5	MDI2_P	
3	MDI3_N	
11	MDI3_P

blocks.text: The connection box converts the custom wrist interface into standard power and Ethernet interfaces. It is typically used with the wrist connector option.
The ports of the connection box are as follows:

rows.text: Connects to the hand via a custom wiring harness
External power input (compatible with provided power adapter)

Use either port C or D independently
Do not connect both ports simultaneously
External power input (compatible with XT30 power connector)

Pin	Signal	Description
1, 2, 3	PWR	Power Input (Positive)
4, 5, 6	GND	Power Input (Negative)
13	FAN_PWR	Fan power supply (6 V, 0.2 A max)
7,8,14	RESERVED	Do not use
15	MDI0_N	1000BASE-T Ethernet
9	MDI0_P	
16	MDI1_N	
10	MDI1_P	
17	MDI2_N	
11	MDI2_P	
18	MDI3_N	
12	MDI3_P

blocks.text: This section describes a typical integration setup using the flange interface. In this example, power and network connections are routed through the robot body, and the robot’s onboard system is ARM-based and runs Ubuntu 22.04.
This section describes how to mount the hand onto the robotic arm using the flange interface.

items.text: Insert the positioning pin (φ6 × 10) into the locating hole at the end of the robotic arm.
Align and mount the flange adapter onto the robot end, ensuring proper engagement with the positioning pin and a flush fit with the mounting surface.
Install the M6 × 10 screws and tighten them in a diagonal (cross) sequence.
Mount the Sharpa Wave hand onto the flange adapter and secure it using the M4 × 12 screws
Connect the hand to the connection box.
Connect the power input to the robot power interface (XT30 or DC 5.5 × 2.5), or use the provided power adapter by plugging it into an AC outlet.
Connect the Ethernet interface:
If the host system has multiple Ethernet ports, connect each connection box directly to a port on the host system.
If the host system has only one Ethernet port, connect all devices via a network switch (host system ↔ switch ↔ connection boxes).
Ensure that the cables are secured along the robot structure.
Avoid interference with robot motion.
Avoid excessive bending or tension.
Ensure Sharpa Wave and the host system (ARM computer) are on the same network segment
Configure the host system IP address according to Section 5.1 (Default Network Settings), e.g., 192.168.10.240
Download the ARM version of the Sharpa Wave SDK
Run sharpa_wave_example following the instructions in README.md

blocks.text: Successful connection is indicated if Sharpa Wave moves.

Integrating a camera at the wrist can significantly enhance perception for manipulation and teleoperation tasks.
This section provides design guidelines for mounting a camera using the wrist interface.
A depth camera such as the Intel RealSense D405 is used as a reference example, but the guidelines apply to similar devices.
Before integration, review the camera datasheet and confirm the following:

items.text: Mechanical mounting interface
Cable type and routing requirements
Field of view (FOV)

blocks.text: These parameters directly affect placement, visibility, and integration complexity.

items.text: Viewing Direction

blocks.text: The camera should be oriented toward the workspace in front of the palm.
A slight downward tilt (approximately 20°) is recommended to:

items.text: improve visibility of contact areas
reduce occlusion from fingers
maintain a stable view during manipulation

blocks.text: The exact angle should be adjusted based on the application.

items.text: Field of View Coverage

blocks.text: The camera should be positioned such that its FOV covers the palm and fingers.

items.text: Use CAD or simple geometric models to approximate the FOV
Verify that key interaction regions are within view
Treat the FOV model as an approximation; validate with real hardware
Cable Routing and Clearance

blocks.text: Cable routing must be considered early in the design:

items.text: Avoid interference with finger motion and workspace
Ensure sufficient clearance from surrounding structures
Provide enough bending space for the cable
along the side of the wrist
along the underside of the wrist

blocks.text: To integrate the camera with the wrist interface, a custom adapter is typically required.

items.text: Adapter Structure

blocks.text: For manufacturability and flexibility, the adapter is commonly divided into two parts:

items.text: a wrist-side component that attaches to the wrist interface
a camera-side component that mounts the camera
Alignment and Repeatability

blocks.text: To ensure consistent camera pose after assembly:

items.text: include precision alignment features (e.g., dowel pins)
avoid relying solely on screw positioning

blocks.text: This is especially important if the adapter is frequently removed and reinstalled.

items.text: Prototyping and Production
Use 3D printing for rapid prototyping and design validation
After validation, use CNC machining for final parts to ensure strength and precision

blocks.text: A successful wrist camera integration should:

items.text: provide stable and unobstructed view of the workspace
avoid interference with hand motion
ensure reliable cable routing
maintain repeatable alignment after assembly

blocks.text: The Sharpa Wave SDK provides a unified programming interface for controlling the dexterous hand, accessing real-time state feedback, and integrating tactile sensing into robotic applications.
It is designed to support both rapid prototyping and production-grade system integration.
The Sharpa Wave SDK is available in both Python and C++, with consistent core abstractions and APIs across languages.

items.text: Python SDK is recommended for:
rapid development and prototyping
algorithm development and research workflows
data processing and machine learning integration
C++ SDK is recommended for:
performance-critical applications
real-time control systems
embedded or production deployment

blocks.text: Both SDKs expose the same core capabilities (device management, motion control, state feedback, and tactile sensing), allowing users to switch between languages with minimal changes.
A typical development workflow is to prototype in Python and migrate to C++ when higher performance or tighter system integration is required.
The SDK provides full access to device functionality:

items.text: Device Management
Discover available devices on the network
Connect / disconnect devices
Query device metadata and status
Motion Control
Joint-level position control
Admittance control (force → motion)
MIT impedance control
Runtime parameter tuning (stiffness, damping, etc.)
State Feedback
Joint angles, velocities, torques (500 Hz)
Device status and fault information
Tactile Sensing
Access multi-modal tactile data:
raw image
deformation map
6-DOF force
contact point
Support both polling and callback-based data pipelines
System Configuration
Control source / control mode switching
Network and device configuration
Runtime parameter adjustment

blocks.text: The SDK follows a manager–device architecture:

items.text: SharpaWaveManager
Singleton entry point
Handles device discovery and connection lifecycle
SharpaWave
Represents a connected hand device
Provides all runtime control and sensing interfaces

blocks.text: This abstraction is consistent across Python and C++.
A typical SDK usage flow is:

items.text: Get the global manager instance
Discover available devices
Connect to a device
Wait until the device is ready
Configure control source and control mode
Send commands or read data
Disconnect cleanly

blocks.text: If tactile sensing is used, the workflow may additionally include:

items.text: waiting for tactile readiness
performing tactile calibration
fetching tactile frames (polling)
processing tactile data via callbacks

blocks.text: Sharpa Pilot and SDK share the same underlying communication interfaces:

items.text: Only one control source can actively send motion commands
Tactile streaming ports are shared
Do not run SDK tactile programs and Sharpa Pilot simultaneously
Ensure control source is switched to SDK before sending commands

blocks.text: Download the Sharpa Wave SDK from: https://www.sharpa.com/pages/downloads
Select the package that matches your CPU architecture (e.g., amd64 or arm64).

items.text: runtime libraries
C++ headers
Python bindings
configuration files and examples

blocks.text: A typical SDK installation provides the following directory layout:
The most commonly used paths are:

items.text: the public C++ header in /usr/include/sharpa-wave-sdk/
the runtime library in /usr/lib/sharpa-wave-sdk/
the Python package in /usr/lib/sharpa-wave-sdk/python/
configuration files and examples in /usr/share/sharpa-wave-sdk/
Python SDK
The Python module is installed automatically by the `sharpa-wave-sdk` Debian package.
The module is located at `/usr/lib/sharpa-wave-sdk/python`.
Install optional dependencies for Python examples:
If you only run tactile visualization examples and prefer system packages, you may install:
C++ SDK
The public C++ header and runtime library are installed automatically by the sharpa-wave-sdk Debian package.
To compile C++ examples, install a C++ build toolchain:
To compile tactile C++ examples, install OpenCV development headers:
Link applications with:

blocks.text: This section provides the shortest recommended path to a working integration.

items.text: power on the Sharpa Wave
connect the host and the device to the same subnet
confirm that the SDK Python module is installed correctly

blocks.text: For physical hardware connection, safety handling, and device startup instructions, refer to the product user manual.

blocks.code:
import sys
import time

sys.path.insert(0, "/usr/lib/sharpa-wave-sdk/python")

from sharpa import ControlMode, ControlSource, SharpaWaveManager

def main() -> int:
    manager = SharpaWaveManager.get_instance()
    time.sleep(1)

    sns = manager.get_all_device_sn()
    if not sns:
        print("No device found")
        return 1

    wave = manager.connect(sns[0])

    if not wave.start():
        print("Failed to start device")
        manager.disconnect_all()
        return 1

    try:
        while not wave.is_hand_ready():
            time.sleep(0.2)

        err_source = wave.set_control_source(ControlSource.SDK)
        err_mode = wave.set_control_mode(ControlMode.POSITION)
        if err_source.code != 0 or err_mode.code != 0:
            print("Failed to initialize control state")
            return 1

        state = wave.get_states()
        print(f"Current joint angles: {state.angles}")

        target_angles_deg = [0.0] * 22
        err = wave.set_joint_position_degree(target_angles_deg)
        if err.code != 0:
            message = getattr(err, "to_string", None)
            print(message() if callable(message) else err.message)
        else:
            print("Command sent: move all joints to 0 degree")

        time.sleep(1)
        return 0
    finally:
        wave.set_control_mode(ControlMode.ZERO_FORCE)
        wave.stop()
        manager.disconnect_all()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

blocks.text: Assuming the SDK has been installed to the default system paths, run the example with:
For more examples, please refer to:
Use the singleton manager to discover and connect devices.
To obtain all discovered serial numbers:
To obtain complete information for all discovered devices:
get_all_devices() returns a list of DeviceInfo structures.
Use this overload when the target serial number is known.

blocks.code:
from sharpa import HandSide

wave = manager.connect(HandSide.RIGHT)

blocks.text: Use this overload when you want to connect by logical hand side instead of serial number.
Use SharpaWaveConfig when tactile behavior or startup behavior must be customized.

blocks.code:
from sharpa import SharpaWaveConfig

config = SharpaWaveConfig()
config.disable_tactile = False
config.disable_sync_time = False
config.tactile_config_file = ""

wave = manager.connect(device_sn, config)

blocks.text: After disconnecting, references to the old SharpaWave object must not be reused.
After connecting, wait until the device is ready before sending commands.
If tactile is enabled, you may also check:
After obtaining a SharpaWave object from SharpaWaveManager::connect(...), the application should call start() before using tactile-related functionality.
Use stop() when hand operation or tactile data transmission and reception need to be stopped without fully disconnecting the device.
After destroy(), internal resources are released. The object reference should be treated as invalid until the manager reconnects the device.
After reboot, wait again until the device becomes ready.
Use get_device_info() when you need structured metadata.
Use get_device_info_json() when you need a serialized JSON representation.

items.text: serial number
firmware version
hand side
device type
IP address
TCP/UDP ports
runtime status

blocks.text: Time synchronization is typically handled during startup unless disabled by configuration.
Changing the device IP address causes a device reboot. After the reboot:

items.text: update host network settings if needed
make sure the host is on the same subnet
reconnect the device

blocks.text: These interfaces can be used to scale the effective current limit and speed limit of the device during operation, without changing the application-level motion logic.
The current coefficient controls the upper scaling applied to joint current output.
Use a smaller coefficient when lower output force or more conservative behavior is desired.
The speed coefficient controls the upper scaling applied to joint angular speed.
Use a smaller coefficient when slower motion is required for testing, demonstration, or safer interaction.
When adjusting current or speed coefficients:

items.text: start from the recommended default values
increase them only when the application has been validated sufficiently
verify the effect in a controlled environment before task execution

blocks.text: For applications with uncertain control quality or incomplete tuning, it is strongly recommended to keep these coefficients conservative. Excessive values may lead to unstable motion, excessive mechanical load, or hardware damage.
Advanced settings can be controlled with a generic JSON interface.
Further parameter definitions will be provided where applicable.
Before motion control, it is necessary to ensure that the state is enabled.

Invoking the set_control_mode or set_control_source interface will automatically transition the device to the enabled state.
During a call to a hardware settings modification interface, the device will automatically switch to the disabled state; upon completion, it will automatically revert to the enabled state.
Applications that drive the hand through the SDK should explicitly set SDK control source.

blocks.code:
from sharpa import ControlSource

wave.set_control_source(ControlSource.SDK)
err, source = wave.get_control_source()

blocks.text: Set the required control mode before sending commands for that mode.

blocks.code:
from sharpa import ControlMode

wave.set_control_mode(ControlMode.POSITION)
err, mode = wave.get_control_mode()

items.text: ZERO_FORCE
POSITION
ADMITTANCE
MIT

blocks.text: To send joint positions in radians:
To send joint positions in degrees:

For all full-hand vector-based motion interfaces, the input vector must follow the SDK-defined joint DOF ordering.
 This applies to position commands and any other APIs that use full-hand joint vectors.
 For the complete ordering definition, refer to Appendix 1 Joint DOF Ordering.

In Admittance mode, the controller regulates the robot's dynamic response to external interaction forces. Admittance control accepts external force as input and generates motion commands (position) as output, emulating a virtual mass-spring-damper system.

items.text: : Measured external force/torque (N·m)
: Virtual mass/inertia (kg·m²)
: Virtual damping (N·m·s/rad)
: Virtual stiffness (N·m/rad)
: Nominal target trajectory (rad)
: Modified reference trajectory (rad)

blocks.text: To utilize this mode, first switch the control mode to ADMITTANCE and establish the nominal target trajectory using the joint position interface:

Once the mode is active, you can configure the virtual dynamic characteristics (mass, damping, and stiffness) for each joint via the parameter setting interface. This allows you to tune the robot's "softness" or "heaviness" during interaction:
In MIT mode, the position loop and velocity loop are arranged in parallel. Their outputs are summed together with the feedforward torque to generate the reference torque  :

items.text: : Reference torque, in N·m
: Position gain
: Velocity gain
: Target joint position, in rad
: Actual joint position, in rad
:Target joint velocity, in rad/s
: Actual joint velocity, in rad/s
: Feedforward torque, in N.m

blocks.code:
wave.set_control_mode(ControlMode.MIT)

pos = [0.0] * 22
vel = [0.0] * 22
tau = [0.0] * 22

err = wave.set_mit_control(pos, vel, tau)

blocks.text: In MIT control mode, a set of default kp and kdparameters is provided. If these defaults do not suit your application, you can use set_parameter interface to modify them.
Note: Improper kp and kdvalues may cause system oscillation.

It is recommended to keep Kd at its default value, and tune joint stiffness by adjusting Kp. The recommended Kp range is as follows(applicable only when torque_source is set to current(default); otherwise, oscillation may occur):
Use get_states() to read the latest joint state packet.

items.text: timestamp
sequence
angles
velocities
torques

blocks.text: Convenience APIs are available for reading positions only.

blocks.code:
err_rad, positions_rad = wave.get_joint_position_rad()
err_deg, positions_deg = wave.get_joint_position_degree()

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave SDK provides tactile data access for supported devices through polling and callback-based interfaces. Tactile data can include raw image data, deformation maps, force-related features, contact-point information, and other structured outputs depending on device capability and runtime configuration.
Before using tactile APIs, check both support and readiness.

items.text: has_tactile_support() indicates whether the connected device supports tactile sensing.
is_tactile_ready() indicates whether the tactile pipeline has been initialized successfully and is ready to provide data.

blocks.text: If tactile is not supported or not ready, tactile frame APIs may fail or return no valid data.

When tactile signals show drift over time, tactile calibration can be used to reset the tactile output to zero. This helps establish a clean reference baseline before tactile sensing is used in a task.
It is recommended to perform tactile calibration before starting a new task, experiment, or data-collection procedure.

items.text: keep the hand stationary
ensure fingertips are not in contact with external objects

blocks.text: To fetch a tactile frame synchronously:

blocks.code:
frame = wave.fetch_tactile_frame(0, 1.0)
if frame is not None:
    raw = frame["content"].get("RAW")
    deform = frame["content"].get("DEFORM")
    f6 = frame["content"].get("F6")
    contact_point = frame["content"].get("CONTACT_POINT")

items.text: channel: tactile channel index. For the definition of tactile channel indices, see Appendix 2, Tactile Channel Mapping.
timeout: maximum wait time in seconds

blocks.text: Users should not assume that every frame always contains every payload. Always check whether a key exists before reading it.

blocks.code:
def tactile_callback(frame):
    if frame is None:
        return

    channel = frame["channel"]
    timestamp = frame["ts"]
    raw = frame["content"].get("RAW")
    deform = frame["content"].get("DEFORM")
    f6 = frame["content"].get("F6")
    contact_point = frame["content"].get("CONTACT_POINT")


wave.set_tactile_callback(tactile_callback)

blocks.text: Callbacks run in the data acquisition path and should remain lightweight. Heavy processing should be offloaded to worker threads or a queue.
In Python, tactile frames are exposed as dictionary-like objects. Common fields include:

items.text: channel: tactile channel index
ts: frame timestamp
content: map from payload name to payload data
shape: map from payload name to shape information

blocks.text: The content field may contain payloads such as:

blocks.code:
raw_jpg = frame["content"].get("RAW_JPG")
raw = frame["content"].get("RAW")
force = frame["content"].get("F6")
deform_jpg = frame["content"].get("DEFORM_JPG")
deform = frame["content"].get("DEFORM")
contact_point = frame["content"].get("CONTACT_POINT")

blocks.text: Typical payloads in frame["content"] may include:
The returned payloads can be converted to NumPy arrays when numerical or image processing is required.

blocks.code:
import numpy as np

raw_data = frame["content"].get("RAW")
if raw_data is not None:
    height = frame["shape"]["RAW"][1]
    width = frame["shape"]["RAW"][2]
    raw_image = np.asarray(raw_data).reshape(height, width).astype(np.uint8)

deform_data = frame["content"].get("DEFORM")
if deform_data is not None:
    height = frame["shape"]["DEFORM"][1]
    width = frame["shape"]["DEFORM"][2]
    deform_image = np.asarray(deform_data).reshape(height, width).astype(np.uint8)

blocks.text: The SDK provides helper functions for mapping tactile image-space information.

blocks.code:
point = wave.deform_map_uv(channel, row, col)
mm = wave.deform_map_value(value_ui8)

blocks.text: These helpers are useful when converting deformation-map coordinates or values into more meaningful geometric or physical interpretations.
This can be used to inspect packet reception quality, dropped data, and runtime tactile streaming behavior.
If tactile startup fails because of host port conflicts, use the recovery helpers:

blocks.code:
ok = wave.retry_tactile_alternate_port()
ports = wave.get_tactile_alternate_ports()
port = wave.get_next_available_tactile_port([])
bound = wave.bind_tactile_port(port)

blocks.text: These APIs help recover tactile functionality when the default host port is occupied or unavailable.
Most SDK operations return either an Error object or a tuple of (Error, value).
An Error with code == 0 indicates that the operation completed successfully. If code != 0, the operation failed, and the message field provides additional diagnostic information.
For the complete list of error codes and their meanings, refer to Appendix 3 Error Codes.
For APIs that return (Error, value), always check the Error object before using the returned value:

items.text: always check the returned Error before continuing
log both Error.code and Error.message during debugging
do not assume that a returned value is valid unless the associated Error indicates success

blocks.text: Use get_fault_code() to inspect current device and software fault conditions.
The returned map associates each FaultCode with a list of relevant indices, if applicable. The meaning of these indices depends on the fault context. For example:

items.text: for motion-related faults, the indices may refer to joint indices
for tactile-related faults, the indices may refer to tactile channels
for some system-level faults, no specific index may apply, and the associated vector may be empty

blocks.text: For the complete list of fault codes and their meanings, refer to Appendix 4 Fault Codes.

items.text: check reported fault codes when device behavior is abnormal
interpret the associated indices according to the subsystem involved
use the reported indices to narrow down motion, tactile, or other subsystem issues when available
the device is powered on
the host and device are on the same subnet
firewall or endpoint security software is not blocking required traffic
heartbeat packets can reach the host
the serial number is correct
the selected hand side uniquely identifies one device
the device is reachable on the network
the SDK runtime library is correctly installed

blocks.text: 3.3.3 The device does not respond to motion commands

items.text: is_hand_ready() returns true
control source is set to SDK
the correct control mode has been selected
the device is enabled
command vectors use the correct DOF order and length
the device supports tactile sensing
tactile is enabled and ready
the host port required for tactile reception is available
the configured host IP and target port are correct
another application is not occupying the tactile port

blocks.text: 3.3.5 Port occupied or tactile bind failure
Use the tactile port recovery helpers:

items.text: retry_tactile_alternate_port()
get_next_available_tactile_port()
bind_tactile_port()

blocks.text: 3.3.6 Device becomes unreachable after IP change

items.text: the device has completed reboot
the host computer is on the same subnet as the new device IP
the application reconnects after the reboot
always log Error.code and Error.message
wait for readiness before issuing commands
log discovered devices and their IP addresses during startup
validate command vector sizes before sending motion commands
keep tactile callbacks lightweight
disconnect cleanly before application exit

In Admittance mode, the controller regulates the robot's dynamic response to external interaction forces. Admittance control accepts external force as input and generates motion commands (position) as output, emulating a virtual mass-spring-damper system.

blocks.code:

blocks.text: Sharpa Wave SDK provides tactile data access for supported devices through polling and callback-based interfaces. Tactile data can include raw image data, deformation maps, force-related features, contact-point information, and other structured outputs depending on device capability and runtime configuration.
Before using tactile APIs, check both support and readiness.

blocks.code:
bool supported = wave.has_tactile_support();
bool ready = wave.is_tactile_ready();

items.text: has_tactile_support() indicates whether the connected device supports tactile sensing.
is_tactile_ready() indicates whether the tactile pipeline has been initialized successfully and is ready to provide data.

blocks.text: If tactile is not supported or not ready, tactile frame APIs may fail or return no valid data.

When tactile signals show drift over time, tactile calibration can be used to reset the tactile output to zero. This helps establish a clean reference baseline before tactile sensing is used in a task.
It is recommended to perform tactile calibration before starting a new task, experiment, or data-collection procedure.

items.text: keep the hand stationary
ensure fingertips are not in contact with external objects

blocks.text: The SDK provides two ways to fetch tactile data synchronously.
To fetch a full tactile frame:
To fetch a compact tactile frame:

items.text: channel: tactile channel index. For the definition of tactile channel indices, see Appendix 2, Tactile Channel Mapping.
timeout: maximum wait time in seconds

blocks.text:
Use fetch_tactile_frame() when you want the most general data representation.
Use fetch_tactile_frame_compact() when you want a simplified structure with commonly used outputs unpacked into vectors.
Callbacks run in the data acquisition path and should remain lightweight. Heavy processing should be offloaded to worker threads or a queue.
The frame data returned by fetch_tactile_frame() or delivered through set_tactile_callback() contains a content field:
Frame::content is a map from payload name to payload data block.

items.text: The key is the name of the tactile data type, such as "RAW", "DEFORM", "F6", or "CONTACT_POINT".
The value is an instance of sharpa::tactile::DataBlock, which stores the underlying tensor-like data buffer together with its shape information.

blocks.text: This means that a tactile frame is not limited to a single image or a single numeric vector. Instead, it is a container of one or more tactile outputs generated for the same frame.
A DataBlock is the generic data representation used for tactile payloads. It provides:

items.text: shape() → dimensions of the payload, for example [1, H, W] or [1, 1, 6]
size() → number of elements
nbytes() → total byte size
data() → raw pointer to the underlying buffer

blocks.code:
auto frame = wave.fetch_tactile_frame(0, 1.0);
if (!frame) return;

auto raw_jpg = frame->content["RAW_JPG"]; // get original image in JPG format
auto raw = frame->content["RAW"];  // get original image in matrix format
auto force  = frame->content["F6"]; // get 6-DOF force
auto deform_jpg = frame->content["DEFORM_JPG"]; // get deformation depth map in JPG format
auto deform = frame->content["DEFORM"]; // get deformation depth map in matrix format
auto contact_point = frame->content["CONTACT_POINT"]; // get contact point in matrix format

blocks.text: Because data() returns a raw pointer, the application must cast it to the correct element type, such as uint8_t* or float*, depending on the payload being read.
Typical payloads in Frame::content may include:
Users should not assume that every frame always contains every payload. Always check whether a key exists before reading it.

A CompactFrame exposes commonly used payloads through direct vectors and shape objects rather than a generic content map. Use CompactFrame when you want a simpler access path to standard tactile outputs, and use the full Frame representation when you need flexible payload lookup.
The SDK provides helper functions for mapping tactile image-space information.

blocks.code:
auto point = wave.deform_map_uv(channel, row, col);
float mm = wave.deform_map_value(value_ui8);

blocks.text: These helpers are useful when converting deformation-map coordinates or values into more meaningful geometric or physical interpretations.
This can be used to inspect packet reception quality, dropped data, and runtime tactile streaming behavior.
If tactile startup fails because of host port conflicts, use the recovery helpers:
These APIs help recover tactile functionality when the default host port is occupied or unavailable.
Most SDK operations return either sharpa::Error or std::pair<Error, T>.

An Error with code == 0 indicates that the operation completed successfully.
 If code != 0, the operation failed, and the message field provides additional diagnostic information.
For the complete list of error codes and their meanings, refer to Appendix 3 Error Codes.
For APIs that return std::pair<Error, T>, always check the Error object before using the returned value:

items.text: always check the returned Error before continuing
log both Error.code and Error.message during debugging
do not assume that a returned value is valid unless the associated Error indicates success

blocks.text: Use get_fault_code() to inspect current device and software fault conditions.

The returned map associates each FaultCode with a list of relevant indices, if applicable.
 The meaning of these indices depends on the fault context. For example:

items.text: for motion-related faults, the indices may refer to joint indices
for tactile-related faults, the indices may refer to tactile channels
for some system-level faults, no specific index may apply, and the associated vector may be empty

blocks.text: For the complete list of fault codes and their meanings, refer to Appendix 4 Fault Codes.

items.text: check reported fault codes when device behavior is abnormal
interpret the associated indices according to the subsystem involved
use the reported indices to narrow down motion, tactile, or other subsystem issues when available

blocks.text: 3.3.5 Port occupied or tactile bind failure
Use the tactile port recovery helpers:

items.text: retry_tactile_alternate_port()
get_next_available_tactile_port()
bind_tactile_port()

blocks.text: 3.3.6 Device becomes unreachable after IP change

items.text: the device has completed reboot
the host computer is on the same subnet as the new device IP
the application reconnects after the reboot
always log Error.code and Error.message
wait for readiness before issuing commands
log discovered devices and their IP addresses during startup
validate command vector sizes before sending motion commands
keep tactile callbacks lightweight
disconnect cleanly before application exit

blocks.text: For vectors that involve all joint DOFs, the elements are ordered as follows:
The tactile channel ranges are organized by hand side as follows:

items.text: wave.fetch_tactile_frame(0, 1.0) means fetching tactile data from right-hand channel 0, with a maximum wait time of 1.0 second.
wave.fetch_tactile_frame(5, 1.0) means fetching tactile data from left-hand channel 5, with a maximum wait time of 1.0 second.
Applications should use the correct tactile channel for the target finger or sensing location.

blocks.text: The following table lists the result codes returned by API functions. These codes primarily focus on the success of communication, data integrity, and protocol validation for individual instructions.

rows.text: Check API arguments (type, length, range) and retry
Check power, IP/port, cables, and firewall; retry
Ensure device is online and active; check command expectations; retry or increase timeout
Reduce request load; retry; restart if needed
Check SDK/firmware compatibility; update or avoid API
Restart device; if persistent, collect logs and contact support
Record logs; restart and retry; contact support if persistent
Ensure device allows parameter saving; retry or contact support
Check cable integrity and protocol version
Check device state (e.g., busy or protected)
Check network link and host TCP/IP
Check network load and device response

blocks.text: The following table provides detailed descriptions of the device’s internal health and safety status. These codes are reported by the firmware to indicate system warnings, hardware failures, or environmental protection triggers.

rows.text: Check power supply; contact support if persistent
Check power supply; power cycle; contact support if persistent
Check power supply; power cycle; contact support if persistent
Power cycle; contact support if persistent
Power cycle; contact support if persistent
Check for external load/collision; contact support if persistent
Check control mode; ignore if in Zero-force manual movement
No action if under load; otherwise contact support
Resend config; power cycle if persistent
Check connection; power cycle; contact support if persistent
Check connection; power cycle; contact support if persistent
Check connection; retry; contact support if persistent
Restart system; contact support if persistent
Restart system; contact support if persistent
Restart system; contact support if persistent
Restart system; contact support if persistent
Restart system; contact support if persistent
Check connections and cables; contact support if persistent

blocks.text: This section helps you quickly diagnose and resolve common issues when using Sharpa Wave. Follow the steps below based on your observed symptom.
When an issue occurs, follow this order:

items.text: Check the indicator light on the back of the hand
Check whether the device appears in Sharpa Pilot
Check ErrorCode (API / communication issues) in Sharpa Pilot
Check FaultCode (hardware / safety issues) in Sharpa Pilot

blocks.text: The back-of-hand indicator provides the fastest way to assess system status.

rows.text: Stop operation immediately and check FaultCode

blocks.text:
Symptom
Sharpa Pilot cannot detect the device.

items.text: Confirm the device has completed startup (wait ~20 seconds)
Check power connection and use the official power adapter
Ensure that the host PC network settings are configured according to Section 5.1, Default Network Settings
Temporarily disable firewall on the host PC
Connect the device directly to the PC (avoid switches/routers)
Check Ethernet cable and port
If IP is unknown, restore factory network settings (press rear button 3 times consecutively)

blocks.text: 3.2 Device Detected but Cannot Be Controlled

Symptom
Device appears in Sharpa Pilot but does not respond to commands.

items.text: Check the Control Source, and Control Mode in Sharpa Pilot.
Look for warning banners in the UI and resolve them.
Check the joint data frequency (about 500 Hz is expected).
If the frequency is 0 Hz, the device may be offline
Temporarily disable firewall and test:
Restart Sharpa Pilot and try again.

blocks.text:
Symptom
A joint does not move or behaves abnormally.

items.text: Check FaultCode for motor-related errors
Power cycle the device
If the issue persists, contact Sharpa Support

blocks.text:
Symptom
Joint does not return to correct zero position.

items.text: Check the current angle in Sharpa Pilot.
If the angle is near 0° but the physical position is incorrect, recalibrate the joints.
If the angle is far from 0°, see Section 4.1, Single Joint Abnormal.

blocks.text:
Symptom
Actual joint motion does not follow commands.

items.text: Ensure that the command frequency is within the supported range.
Avoid large step changes greater than 20° per frame.
Check for hardware issues if only one joint is affected.
If multiple joints are affected, contact Sharpa Support.
Increase the joint current coefficient in Sharpa Pilot within the range of 0.5 to 1.0.
Check for thermal warnings (see Section 6.2).
Allow the device to cool down if overheating is present.

blocks.text:
Symptom
No response in tactile visualization or SDK data.

items.text: Check the FaultCode in Sharpa Pilot.
Verify the network connection by pinging the device IP address.
Confirm that the IP address and subnet settings are correct.
Use Wireshark to verify the UDP data stream on port 50001.
Confirm that all five tactile sensors are transmitting.

blocks.text:
Symptom
Non-zero force or deformation without contact.

items.text: Perform tactile calibration in Sharpa Pilot
Power cycle the device.
Ensure that no contact occurs within the first 20 seconds after startup.

blocks.text:
Symptom
Tactile visualization is slow or <30 Hz.

items.text: Verify that the Ethernet link speed is Gigabit.
Check the Ethernet speed by running:
Confirm that the reported speed is 1000Mb/s.
Check the device temperature and cooling condition
Indicates command success or communication issues
Example causes:
Invalid parameters
Network failure
Protocol mismatch
Commands fail
SDK/API returns errors
Errorcodes are reported in Sharpa Pilot

blocks.text: For the full list of ErrorCodes, refer to the Sharpa Wave SDK .

items.text: Indicates hardware or safety status
Example causes:
Overcurrent / overvoltage
Motor or sensor failure
Overtemperature
Indicator light is yellow/red
Device behaves abnormally
Faultcodes are reported in Sharpa Pilot

blocks.text: For the full list of FaultCodes, refer to the Sharpa Wave SDK .
Use the following actions to resolve most common issues.
Try the following steps in order:

items.text: Retry the operation
Restart Sharpa Pilot
Power cycle the device
Recalibrate joints or tactile sensors
Check cables and network configuration

blocks.text: The back button supports quick restart and configuration reset.

items.text: Press 2 times: Restart the device
Press 3 times: Reset configuration and restart

blocks.text:
Tip:
A natural “double-click” or “triple-click” rhythm works well.
A configuration reset restores the following settings:

items.text: Network Settings
Control and System Parameters
Control mode
Control source
Motor current / speed coefficients
Admittance control parameters
MIT control parameters
If the presses are too slow, the action may not be triggered
Reset will overwrite current network and control configurations
Use reset only when necessary (e.g., device not reachable)
A fault persists after 2 power cycles
Critical faults (flashing red) occur
Motor / encoder / sensor errors are reported
Calibration fails or parameters cannot be loaded
Unknown or undocumented fault codes appear

blocks.text: This notice applies to the use of this product, unless otherwise specified in a separate written agreement.

Users are advised to thoroughly read and understand this legal notice before using the product. Continued use of the product constitutes acceptance of and agreement to be bound by the following terms.

items.text: No guarantees

blocks.text: Unless otherwise agreed in writing, this product is provided “as is”, without any express or implied warranties, including but not limited to warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, uninterrupted operation, or performance.

The user understands and agrees that, as this product is under continuous development, Sharpa reserves the right to modify its functionality, structure, or performance at any time, and makes no guarantees regarding the product's stability or expected results in any specific application scenario.
Confirm product development maturity with Sharpa if needed.

items.text: Prohibited uses

blocks.text: Unless otherwise explicitly authorized in writing, the user shall not use this product for any of the following purposes, including but not limited to:

items.text: Critical mission systems (such as the main control systems in industrial automation)
Traffic safety control systems (such as autonomous driving decision-making modules)
Medical devices or life-support systems
Any other scenarios where failure could result in personal injury or death, significant property damage, or risks to public safety
Limitation of liability

blocks.text: To the maximum extent permitted by applicable law, Sharpa shall not be liable for any direct, indirect, incidental, special, or consequential losses (including but not limited to data loss, business interruption, or loss of profits) arising from the use or inability to use this product, provided that Sharpa has not engaged in gross negligence or willful misconduct.

items.text: Export control and sanctions compliance

blocks.text: This product and its related technologies may be subject to applicable export control and sanctions regulations of relevant jurisdictions. The user shall comply with all such regulations and shall not export, re-export, or transfer this product to any sanctioned or embargoed countries, regions, individuals, entities, military end-users, or law enforcement agencies. Users shall also refrain from using this product for military end use or any other prohibited end-uses.

blocks.text: This product, including its software, firmware, design, appearance, and technical documentation, is the property of Sharpa or its licensors, and is protected by copyright law, patent law, and other applicable laws. Without prior written permission from Sharpa, users may not reproduce, modify, distribute, decompile, reverse engineer, or use it for commercial purposes in any form.

Sharpa Wave and its logo are registered trademarks of Sharpa. All other trademarks, service marks, and company names in this manual or on Sharpa's official website are the property of their respective owners.

items.text: Warranty terms

blocks.text: Sharpa warrants that its products and accessories, as manufactured and delivered, are free from defects in materials and workmanship, and conform to the specifications agreed in writing. Unless otherwise specified in writing, Sharpa provides a warranty period of twelve (12) months, starting from the date of delivery.

During the warranty period, if you discover any defects in the product, please contact Sharpa technical support in writing. Upon confirmation that the issue falls within the scope of the warranty, Sharpa will repair or replace the product free of charge.

items.text: Exclusions

blocks.text: The following cases are NOT covered by Sharpa's warranty service, and any resulting responsibilities and costs shall be borne by the user:

items.text: Disassembly, modification, or repair of the product without Sharpa’s authorization, whether by the user or a third party
Failure to reasonably store, install, operate, or maintain the product in accordance with industry best practices and the relevant instructions provided in this manual, resulting in malfunctions
Malfunctions caused by exceeding the inherent operating limits of the product or its accessories
Revision notice

blocks.text:
Sharpa reserves the right to update, modify, or replace the contents of this manual, and to release the latest version without prior notice.
It is recommended that users contact Sharpa to obtain the latest version of this manual before using the product. Unless otherwise agreed, the latest version shall prevail.
Do not disassemble, repair, modify, or alter the product by yourself or through any third party without Sharpa’s explicit written consent, except for replacing fingertip accessories.
Unauthorized disassembly, repair, modification, or alteration:

items.text: may result in product damage, property loss, and/or personal injury;
constitutes a breach of warranty.

blocks.text: For repair-related support, contact Sharpa or an authorized Sharpa service provider.

items.text: Follow all instructions in this manual.
Failure to comply may result in product damage, property loss, personal injury, and/or a breach of warranty.
Seek technical support when needed at support@sharpa.com.

blocks.text: After the product is upgraded, obtain the latest version of this manual.

items.text: Contact your Sharpa sales representative.
Contact Sharpa technical support at support@sharpa.com.

blocks.text: 2.3 When Integrating the Product into Your Application

items.text: Provide this manual, or access to it, to the intended users of your product.
This dexterous hand is intended to be used as a component of an end product.
The end-product supplier is responsible for:
assessing the risks of use in accordance with applicable standards;
informing intended users of safety-related information.
